generationNote: Cached outputs generated by Claude (Fable 5) against llm_evidence/prompts/evidence_extractor_system.md. The public demo stays static and key-less by design (Decision Log 009). Regenerate against a live API with: python3 llm_evidence/llm_adapter.py --provider anthropic --regenerate (requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY). Span texts are verbatim quotes; offsets are resolved and verified at load time by the same faithfulness check applied to the deterministic extractor. fpShowcase entries analyze real false positives from the error analysis; baseline verdicts shown beside them come from the live model, not the cache.

fraud_elon_multiplier.reviewerSummary: Classic doubling scam: impersonated celebrity authority, a direct transfer request, an impossible instant return, and a countdown. All four signals are quoted verbatim; no benign context present.

spanTexts: Send 0.1 ETH to the verified wallet below
transfer_ask.summary: Direct request to send crypto to a wallet.

missingContext: landing-page behavior of secure-eth-airdrop.xyz is not observable from text
fraud_wallet_drainer_airdrop.reviewerSummary: Drainer-pattern lure: connect-wallet instruction tied to a free-ETH claim on an unverified domain with an expiry window. Domain behavior itself is unobservable, but the ask pattern alone meets the policy's strong-evidence bar.

wallet_connection_ask.summary: Instructs users to connect wallets on an external site.

urgency.summary: Two urgency cues bracketing the ask.

fraud_recovery_fee_advance.reviewerSummary: Advance-fee recovery scam: universal recovery claim plus an upfront BTC fee with same-day promise. This typology re-victimizes people who already lost funds, which raises its severity under the enforcement matrix.

spanTexts: Our verified recovery team can restore any wallet
Pay the processing fee in BTC upfront

fraud_hyip_guaranteed_returns.reviewerSummary: HYIP template: a guarantee, a risk-free claim, an AI-authority gloss, a deposit ask, and a midnight deadline. Every element of the guaranteed-return typology is present in one post.

fraud_fake_exchange_support.reviewerSummary: Support-impersonation phishing: claims exchange authority, demands a deposit disguised as verification, and coerces with an account restriction. Real exchange support never requests deposits — the ask itself is dispositive.

fraud_promo_code_fee.reviewerSummary: Prize/fee scam: an unearned 2 BTC windfall gated behind a small upfront fee — the advance-fee template in giveaway costume.

spanTexts: Send the small activation fee to the wallet address

fraud_fake_grant.reviewerSummary: Brand-impersonation grant scam: corporate authority claim, a BTC payment recast as identity validation, and an immediate bonus. Legitimate grants do not charge validation fees in crypto.

missingContext: the platform being promoted is only revealed in DMs
fraud_dm_investment_mentor.reviewerSummary: Mentor-funnel scam: daily-profit claim plus a DM funnel ending in deposit instructions. The recruitment surface of the fake-platform typology; the destination platform is deliberately kept off-post.

spanTexts: I will show you where to deposit first

legit_coinbase_earnings.reviewerSummary: Ordinary earnings commentary. Mentions an exchange by name with no ask, no promise, and no funnel — the reason brand mentions alone must never be actionable.

spanTexts: Coinbase earnings are out. Revenue was up this quarter

legit_regulator_warning.reviewerSummary: Consumer warning that quotes scam vocabulary in order to warn against it. Labeling this would punish exactly the speech the platform most needs. Stance detection, not keyword detection, is what separates this from promotion.

promised_return.summary: Scam vocabulary quoted in a warning stance.

benign_context.summary: Explicit protective guidance citing a regulator.

legit_academic_research.reviewerSummary: Research description of scam mechanics. Names every scam feature while asking for nothing — protected research context.

spanTexts: Our paper studies how crypto giveaway scams reuse celebrity names

legit_wallet_help.reviewerSummary: Help-seeking about evaluating recovery services safely — the question form of the recovery topic, from a potential victim. Enforcement here would silence the people the policy protects.

spanTexts: evaluate recovery services without getting scammed
recovery_claim.summary: Recovery topic raised as a safety question, not an offer.

legit_satire_bitcoin_ceo.reviewerSummary: Satire of doubling scams using explicitly imaginary assets and a self-negating authority claim. No real funds are solicited.

spanTexts: I will send back two imaginary coins
I am obviously not the CEO of Bitcoin

legit_dev_wallet_bug.reviewerSummary: Developer debugging note. 'Wallet connection' appears in a technical stance with a testnet scope and no user-facing ask.

spanTexts: Debugging a wallet connection bug in our testnet app
The issue is a stale session token
benign_context.summary: Engineering detail inconsistent with a lure.

legit_price_discussion.reviewerSummary: Price commentary with an explicit disclaimer and no ask. Ordinary financial speech.

spanTexts: I am still not treating this as investment advice

ambiguous_airdrop_announcement.reviewerSummary: Third-party description of an upcoming airdrop with the key evidence (the link) explicitly unverified. Could be a legitimate project or a drainer funnel; the text cannot decide it. Route to review — labeling would be premature, ignoring would be negligent.

spanTexts: early users can claim an airdrop next week
I have not verified the link yet

riskFactors: contingency-fee claim is a known scam variant
ambiguous_recovery_service.reviewerSummary: A question about a contingency-fee recovery service. Contingency framing is a documented scam variant, but the speaker is asking, not offering. Review can check whether the named service recurs in the campaign graph.

spanTexts: they charge only after funds are recovered

riskFactors: premium-signal funnel is a pump-group hallmark
ambiguous_influencer_signal.reviewerSummary: Skeptical description of a paid-signals group. The described product matches the pump-group typology, but the speaker is evaluating, not promoting. Reviewer should assess the group, not the poster — the distinction the error analysis shows the baseline misses.

spanTexts: premium members get early signals and better entry prices
I cannot tell if it is legit

ambiguous_qr_image_ocr.reviewerSummary: OCR fragment with a wallet-connect requirement and QR indirection but no observable source or destination. The ask pattern is drainer-like; the evidence quality is too degraded for a public label. Exactly what the review tier exists for.

spanTexts: Claim your bonus token using the QR code

test-fp-144.analysis: The speaker is evaluating a platform's claims, not making them: 'claims to offer', 'I can't find their official website'. The scam being described may well deserve investigation - the person describing it does not. Route the DESCRIBED platform to review; take no action against the poster.

test-fp-045.analysis: Every risk phrase here is quoted from the platform being doubted ('they claim to be registered', 'I haven't verified'). Punishing the doubter teaches users to stop reporting suspicious platforms. The correct object of review is the platform, not the post.

test-fp-146.analysis: An 80% annual return claim is being relayed with explicit doubt ('no regulatory filings visible'). The stance markers - hedging, absence of any ask from the speaker - separate reportage from promotion. A keyword system cannot see this; stance reading can.
